title: Hedge Hogs: The Cowboy Traders Behind Wall Street's Largest Hedge Fund Disaster
excerpt: The dramatic story of Amaranth Advisors' $6.6 billion collapse in 2006 — the largest hedge fund failure in history — and the cowboy traders whose massive natural gas bets brought down the firm.
keyIdeas: Brian Hunter, a single trader at Amaranth, made enormous concentrated bets in natural gas futures
Hunter's trading was essentially gambling on weather patterns and supply disruptions
Amaranth's risk controls failed to constrain Hunter because his bets were so large they corrupted the system
The rivalry between Hunter and John Arnold (Centaurus Energy) escalated into a market-moving battle
keyTakeaways: Even large, sophisticated hedge funds can be destroyed by a single rogue trader
Concentrated position limits and robust risk management are essential safeguards
The natural gas market is uniquely susceptible to manipulation by large traders
Pension funds and endowments suffered significant losses from Amaranth's collapse
whoShouldRead: Risk managers and compliance officers at hedge funds
Pension fund trustees and institutional investors
Traders in commodities and energy markets
Readers who enjoy financial disaster narratives
whoShouldSkip: Readers seeking successful hedge fund strategies to emulate
Those who prefer broad industry histories over focused case studies
People looking for quantitative trading approaches
